table_of_contents.detailed_summary: The lecture begins with a review of Vision Transformers (ViT). Images are split into patches, which are linearly projected into D-dimensional vectors (tokens). Positional embeddings are added to retain spatial awareness before passing the tokens through standard Transformer encoder layers. For classification, a special 'class token' is often prepended, or Global Average Pooling is applied to the output tokens. The instructor also highlights several modern architectural tweaks that have become standard since the original Transformer paper: Pre-Norm (moving layer normalization inside the residual connections for training stability), RMSNorm (a computationally simpler normalization layer), SwiGLU (using gated feed-forward networks for higher-dimensional nonlinearities without increasing total parameters), and Mixture of Experts (MoE) (routing tokens to a subset of parallel 'expert' MLP layers to massively increase parameter count and model capacity with only a modest increase in compute cost).
key_takeaways: Vision Transformers process images by treating image patches as a sequence of tokens, relying on positional embeddings for spatial context.
Modern transformer architectures utilize tweaks like Pre-Norm, RMSNorm, SwiGLU, and Mixture of Experts (MoE) to improve training stability, efficiency, and overall model capacity.

table_of_contents.detailed_summary: The instructor outlines four core computer vision tasks: Image Classification, Semantic Segmentation, Object Detection, and Instance Segmentation. The focus shifts to Semantic Segmentation, which requires assigning a class label to every pixel in an image without distinguishing between separate instances of the same object class. A naive sliding-window CNN approach is discussed but dismissed as computationally infeasible due to the lack of feature reuse across overlapping patches. The solution is Fully Convolutional Networks (FCNs), which replace dense layers with convolutions, allowing the network to output a spatial map of predictions all at once. However, because standard CNNs heavily downsample spatial dimensions to increase the receptive field, FCNs must incorporate an upsampling phase to restore the feature map back to the original image resolution.
key_takeaways: Semantic segmentation assigns a class label to every pixel but does not differentiate between individual object instances.
Fully Convolutional Networks (FCNs) efficiently segment images by using only convolutional layers, avoiding the massive computational overhead of sliding-window approaches.

table_of_contents.detailed_summary: To solve the resolution drop in FCNs, various upsampling techniques are introduced. Non-learnable methods include Nearest Neighbor, 'Bed of Nails', and Max Unpooling (which cleverly reuses the spatial indices saved during the max-pooling phase in the encoder to precisely place activations in the decoder). Learnable upsampling is achieved via Transposed Convolution, which works by multiplying the input scalar by a filter and summing the overlapping regions in the output, effectively acting as a convolution with a fractional stride. The instructor highlights the U-Net architecture, a highly influential fully convolutional network characterized by its U-shape. U-Net's defining feature is the use of skip connections that concatenate high-resolution feature maps from the downsampling path directly to the corresponding layers in the upsampling path, preserving crucial spatial details that would otherwise be lost.
key_takeaways: Transposed convolutions allow networks to learn how to optimally upsample spatial dimensions, unlike fixed unpooling methods.
U-Net remains a dominant architecture for segmentation tasks (especially in medical imaging) due to its skip connections that perfectly align high-resolution context with high-level semantics.

table_of_contents.topic_title: Object Detection and the R-CNN Family
table_of_contents.detailed_summary: Object Detection goes beyond classification by also localizing objects using bounding boxes (predicting x, y, width, height). While single-object detection can be treated as a multitask regression/classification problem, multi-object detection requires handling a variable number of outputs. The R-CNN family evolved to solve this using region proposals. R-CNN cropped pre-computed proposals and ran a CNN on each (very slow). Fast R-CNN ran the CNN once on the whole image and used RoI (Region of Interest) Pooling to extract features for each proposal, drastically speeding up the process but remaining bottlenecked by external proposal generation. Faster R-CNN solved this by introducing the Region Proposal Network (RPN), which uses the CNN's own feature map to predict proposals natively via anchor boxes. The instructor also notes the flaw of RoI Pooling ('snapping' coordinates to discrete grid cells, causing misalignment) and mentions RoI Align as the solution using bilinear interpolation.
key_takeaways: Object detection is framed as a multitask learning problem combining classification (cross-entropy) and bounding box localization (L2/L1 regression).
Faster R-CNN achieves end-to-end object detection by utilizing a shared convolutional backbone for both the Region Proposal Network (RPN) and the final classification/regression heads.

table_of_contents.detailed_summary: To overcome the computational heaviness of two-stage detectors, single-stage detectors like YOLO (You Only Look Once) were developed. YOLO divides the input image into a fixed S x S grid. Each grid cell is responsible for predicting B bounding boxes (including confidence scores and offsets) and C class probabilities directly. This eliminates the region proposal step entirely, reframing detection as a single dense regression problem. While historically slightly less accurate than two-stage methods for small objects, YOLO is extremely fast and scalable, making it the standard for real-time industrial and robotics applications.
key_takeaways: YOLO frames object detection as a single-pass regression problem over a fixed spatial grid, eliminating the need for a separate Region Proposal Network.
Single-stage detectors prioritize real-time inference speed while maintaining competitive accuracy.

table_of_contents.detailed_summary: DETR (Detection Transformer) presents a paradigm shift by treating object detection as a direct set prediction problem using Transformers. A CNN backbone extracts image features, which are flattened, augmented with positional encodings, and fed into a Transformer Encoder. A Transformer Decoder then takes N learned 'object queries' (e.g., 100 queries if the model predicts up to 100 objects) and cross-attends to the encoder features. The output tokens are passed through a feed-forward network to predict class labels and bounding boxes. DETR uses a bipartite matching loss to optimally align predicted boxes with ground truth boxes during training, completely removing the need for heuristic components like anchor boxes or Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS).
key_takeaways: DETR simplifies the detection pipeline by utilizing transformer attention mechanisms and learned object queries instead of hand-crafted anchors.
Bipartite matching loss ensures a one-to-one mapping between predictions and ground truths, removing the need for Non-Maximum Suppression.

table_of_contents.detailed_summary: Instance Segmentation bridges semantic segmentation and object detection by assigning pixel-level masks while distinguishing between individual object instances. The Mask R-CNN architecture extends Faster R-CNN by adding a third branch parallel to the classification and bounding box regression branches. For every proposed Region of Interest (RoI), this fully convolutional branch predicts a binary mask representing the object's precise spatial layout. The instructor emphasizes that RoI Align is absolutely critical for Mask R-CNN, as the quantization errors of standard RoI Pool would destroy the precise pixel-to-pixel alignment necessary for generating accurate masks.
key_takeaways: Instance segmentation differentiates between separate objects of the same class at the pixel level.
Mask R-CNN effectively solves instance segmentation by adding a parallel mask-prediction branch to the Faster R-CNN architecture, relying heavily on RoI Align for spatial accuracy.

table_of_contents.detailed_summary: The final section covers techniques to interpret and understand what Neural Networks learn. While visualizing first-layer weights yields interpretable Gabor-like filters, deeper layers require different techniques. Saliency Maps via backpropagation compute the gradient of the target class score with respect to the input image pixels, revealing which pixels most strongly influence the prediction. Class Activation Mapping (CAM) provides smoother, more robust heatmaps by taking a weighted sum of the final convolutional feature maps, but requires a specific Global Average Pooling architecture. Grad-CAM generalizes this concept to arbitrary CNNs by using the gradients of the class score to weight the feature maps. Finally, Vision Transformers offer inherent interpretability, as one can directly visualize the self-attention and cross-attention weights to see exactly what image patches the model focuses on for specific predictions.
key_takeaways: Saliency maps identify critical pixels by calculating the gradient of the class score with respect to the input image.
Grad-CAM provides generalized, interpretable class activation heatmaps for any CNN by weighting feature maps using gradients, while Transformers offer native interpretability through attention maps.
